title: Snort IDS Threat Detection on OT Networks
app: `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) · `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905)
spl:
index=nozomi sourcetype="nozomi:alert" type_id="SIGN*"
| eval severity_label=case(risk>=8, "Critical", risk>=6, "High", risk>=4, "Medium", risk<4, "Low")
| stats count as hits, values(name) as signatures, dc(dst_ip) as targets by src_ip, severity_label
| where severity_label IN ("Critical", "High")
| sort -hits
| table src_ip, severity_label, hits, targets, signatures
description: OT security platforms include built-in IDS engines with threat intelligence updates to detect known threats — malware, C2 traffic, exploit attempts — crossing into OT networks. This provides signature-based threat detection without deploying separate IDS appliances in sensitive OT environments.
value: OT security platforms include built-in IDS engines with threat intelligence updates to detect known threats — malware, C2 traffic, exploit attempts — crossing into OT networks. This provides signature-based threat detection without deploying separate IDS appliances in sensitive OT environments.
implementation: **Cyber Vision:** Enable Snort IDS on sensors (4GB RAM required); configure Talos subscription for weekly rule updates. **Nozomi:** Guardian includes built-in threat intelligence with Nozomi Threat Intelligence updates. Both platforms provide signature-based IDS for OT. Forward events to Splunk. Correlate with IT security events in Splunk ES for unified threat detection. Alert SOC on Critical/High severity IDS hits targeting OT assets.
visualization: IDS alert timeline; top source IPs table; signature hit frequency chart; OT target heatmap.
knownFalsePositives: IDS hits from test traffic, inline lab sensors, or overlapping IT broadcast domains.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) · `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905).
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:syslog` · Nozomi: `sourcetype=nozomi:alert` (type_id="SIGN").
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
**Cyber Vision:** Enable Snort IDS on sensors (4GB RAM required); configure Talos subscription for weekly rule updates. **Nozomi:** Guardian includes built-in threat intelligence with Nozomi Threat Intelligence updates. Both platforms provide signature-based IDS for OT. Forward events to Splunk. Correlate with IT security events in Splunk ES for unified threat detection. Alert SOC on Critical/High severity IDS hits targeting OT assets.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=nozomi sourcetype="nozomi:alert" type_id="SIGN*"
| eval severity_label=case(risk>=8, "Critical", risk>=6, "High", risk>=4, "Medium", risk<4, "Low")
| stats count as hits, values(name) as signatures, dc(dst_ip) as targets by src_ip, severity_label
| where severity_label IN ("Critical", "High")
| sort -hits
| table src_ip, severity_label, hits, targets, signatures
```

Understanding this SPL

**Snort IDS Threat Detection on OT Networks** — OT security platforms include built-in IDS engines with threat intelligence updates to detect known threats — malware, C2 traffic, exploit attempts — crossing into OT networks. This provides signature-based threat detection without deploying separate IDS appliances in sensitive OT environments.

Documented **Data sources**: `sourcetype=cisco:cybervision:syslog` · Nozomi: `sourcetype=nozomi:alert` (type_id="SIGN"). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): `Cisco Cyber Vision Splunk Add-On` (Splunkbase 5748) · `Nozomi Networks Universal Add-on` (Splunkbase 6905). The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: nozomi; **sourcetype**: nozomi:alert. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=nozomi, sourcetype="nozomi:alert". Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `eval` defines or adjusts **severity_label** — often to normalize units, derive a ratio, or prepare for thresholds.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by src_ip, severity_label** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Filters the current rows with `where severity_label IN ("Critical", "High")` — typically the threshold or rule expression for this monitoring goal.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.
• Pipeline stage (see **Snort IDS Threat Detection on OT Networks**): table src_ip, severity_label, hits, targets, signatures

Step 3 — Validate
Run the search for a time range that should include both routine and change activity, then compare the same assets, flows, and alerts in the Cisco Cyber Vision UI and in Nozomi Guardian or CMC, matching whichever source the event came from. Reconcile severities, counts, and key fields in both tools.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: IDS alert timeline; top source IPs table; signature hit frequency chart; OT target heatmap.
grandmaExplanation: We use signature-based detection in OT so known bad patterns in front of our process networks surface in one place with context.
